Medical literature confirms sella turcica is a real anatomical structure: saddle-shaped depression in the sphenoid bone at the skull base, contains the pituitary gland. Verified from IMAIOS e-Anatomy, ScienceDirect, Wikipedia, and PMC medical journals

Standard medical imaging terminology verified from multiple sources (3D Slicer documentation, e-Anatomy, Radiology references): Axial (transverse/horizontal), Sagittal (left-right), Coronal (front-back) are the three orthogonal planes used for CT/MRI visualization

pi_items.verified_value: Official InVesalius v3.0 release (released April 10, 2017) on GitHub
pi_items.source: GitHub invesalius/invesalius3 repository, v3.0 tag at https://github.com/invesalius/invesalius3/releases/tag/v3.0

InVesalius is documented as legitimate open-source medical imaging software maintained since 2007, licensed under GPL-2.0, used for 3D reconstruction from DICOM images. Confirmed at https://invesalius.github.io/ and https://github.com/invesalius/invesalius3

privileged_info_summary: The task uses the CT Cranium dataset from the official InVesalius v3.0 GitHub release (0051.zip, ~24.3MB), which is real DICOM medical imaging data, not synthetic. The sella turcica is a verified anatomical structure: a saddle-shaped depression in the sphenoid bone at the skull base containing the pituitary gland. The three orthogonal viewing planes (Axial, Sagittal, Coronal) are standard medical imaging terminology. The task expects three PNG exports of at least 10KB each from these orthogonal views. Anti-fraud measures include PNG magic byte validation, file modification timestamp checking, and VLM-based trajectory analysis to verify navigation through the viewing panels.
